_meta.note: LOCKED STANDARD — Armagh is the reference build for the PULSE/26 records spine, confirmed by Rory on 2026-06-30. Built from gaa.ie match reports, Irish Examiner, Irish News, RTE, The42, cross-checked against the championship draw structure on Wikipedia. Two-pointer fields audited and corrected; null left honest where unsourced rather than guessed. This is the pattern the remaining 31 county files should follow.
_meta.verification: All championship scorelines and venues verified against primary match reports. Two-pointer counts audited and corrected against full scorer lists where available (league/R7, Down SF, Derry R1, Louth R2A, Kerry R3); left honestly null for the three games where no reliable full scorer breakdown was sourced (Tyrone, Fermanagh, Monaghan finals — see fields_not_found). League campaign verified for the regulation-season outcome (survived relegation via Round 7 draw with Kerry) but NOT yet built out round-by-round. Manager note sourced. Important finding: the per_county_canonical_clean.json file's Armagh and Cavan rows appear to be corrupted by a scraping artifact (a 'Cavan v Armagh' Round 1 fixture that never happened — the real Round 1 was Armagh v Derry). This draft does not inherit from that file.

manager.name: Kieran McGeeney
manager.note: Long-serving manager since 2014. Led Armagh to a fourth consecutive Ulster final in 2026, breaking a run of three straight final defeats (2023 on penalties to Derry, 2024 on penalties to Donegal, 2025 after extra-time to Donegal) to win the county's first Ulster title since 2008.

source.snippet: This was Armagh's fourth Ulster final in a row, losing the last three by the tightest of margins. In 2023, Derry secured the title on penalties, in 2024, Donegal needed spot-kicks to take the Anglo Celt Cup and last year, the Tir Chonaill county again came out on top, but this time by a single point after extra-time.

league.note: Survived relegation on the final round of the regulation season, drawing 1-21 to 0-24 away to Kerry at Box-IT Athletic Grounds on March 22nd. Exact final table position not yet sourced — flagged as a gap, not guessed.

source.snippet: Armagh drew with Kerry on a scoreline of 1-21 to 0-24 in Round 7 of the Allianz Football League Division 1 on Sunday March 22nd at Box-IT Athletic Grounds Armagh... Kerry will face Donegal in the Allianz Football League Division One final next week after battling out a thrilling draw against Armagh, who survived being relegated.
In 2026, Ulster champions Armagh and Leinster champions Westmeath were knocked out in Round 3 (9th-12th place), meaning only 1 provincial champion (Kerry) played in the last eight.

scorers.note: 3 frees, 1 two-pointer; set up McCormack's extra-time goal
goal at the start of extra time
ran the length of the field for a point on the run

scorers.note: 1 two-point free, 1 free; palmed a second-half goal
palmed to the net after a flowing move

source.snippet: Ulster Senior Football Championship Quarter-Final Armagh 2-32 Fermanagh 1-24 By Gareth Cauldwell at Brewster Park

results.note: Two-pointers mentioned in the match report for both sides but no reliable team total sourced — the '5' figure cited elsewhere was McGurn's personal tally, not Fermanagh's team total, since O'Hanlon and Horan are also reported scoring two-pointers. Left null rather than guessed; needs a full scorer list.

scorers.source_note: The42 full block; Jarly Og Burns 0-5 per gaa.ie report (vs The42 0-4) reconciles the return to the official 3-33

scorers.note: substitute; two-pointer with his first touch on championship debut

source.snippet: Ulster SFC Semi-Final Armagh 3-33 Down 0-14 By Steve Malone at St Tiernach's Park, Clones. Armagh inflicted their biggest ever championship win over neighbours Down.

results.note: Armagh's highest-ever Ulster SFC tally (3-33/42 points); winning margin of 28 equalled the worst defeat ever handed out in the province. Down's heaviest-ever championship loss. Two-pointer count (3 for Armagh: D. McMullan, O. O'Neill, A. O'Neill; 0 for Down) read from the full team sheet in the source, which lists more scorers than the headline five above.

scorers.note: Footballer of the Year contender; won the ball for the killer extra-time goal
squeezed home the decisive extra-time goal under Beggan

source.snippet: Armagh 2-28 Monaghan 0-25: Armagh are Ulster champions after a convincing extra-time win over Monaghan. Armagh have been named the 2026 Ulster Senior Football champions after an extra-time win over Monaghan in Clones.

results.note: First Ulster final meeting of these counties since 1938. Armagh's first Ulster title since 2008, their fourth final in a row after three straight defeats on penalties/extra-time.

source.snippet: Armagh are one game away from an All-Ireland quarter-final after a confident five-point win over disappointing Derry... Armagh led by six points at the break.

results.note: Armagh reduced to 14 men in the second half after a black card for Jason Duffy.

source.snippet: Sam Mulroy, in the very last second of the game, stole a remarkable All-Ireland SFC win for Louth with a quite incredible goal, securing their quarter-final place and leaving the Ulster champions facing an unexpected backdoor battle.

results.note: Sam Mulroy's stoppage-time two-pointer was dropped into the net by Armagh goalkeeper Ethan Rafferty in the final seconds. Already covered in the completed Pulse Panels chapter (Louth v Armagh).

source.snippet: Deadly Kerry put Armagh to the sword with ease... It was a 13-point mauling over this year's Ulster champions where the Kingdom were gifted a goal only a split second before the break before totally owning the second half.

results.note: Elimination game. Armagh's season ends at the Round 3 stage (9th-12th place finish). Kerry's two-pointer count (4: D. Clifford x3 — 2 from play, 1 free — and D. Geaney x1) read from the same gaa.ie report; Kerry's own scorers aren't listed here since this file tracks Armagh's perspective.

collection_meta.notes: This file deliberately follows the Dublin reference file's structure. Found and fixed a data-integrity issue: per_county_canonical_clean.json appears to have a scraping artifact where Cavan's and Armagh's Round 1 rows got cross-contaminated (showing a 'Cavan v Armagh' fixture that never happened). The real Round 1 was Armagh v Derry; Cavan's Round 1 game was against Westmeath (AET). Worth checking whether this same scrambling affected other counties' rows before trusting that file further.
